macro_segments.title: The Debate of Rava and Rav Chisda on Cooking from Yom Tov to Weekday and the Principle of Ho'il

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Debate: Cooking from Yom Tov to Weekday and the Question of Lashes
Rava's Position: The Principle of Ho'il (Since) Allows Cooking if Theoretically Edible Now
Rav Chisda's Position: Intention to Eat Today is Required, Not Theoretical Possibility
The Gemara's Challenge to Rava: The Furrow Analogy and the Problem of Non-Food Acts

macro_segments.title: The Mishna on Five Lashes for Cooking Forbidden Foods on Yom Tov and the Issue of Multiple Categories

micro_segments.title: The Mishna's List of Five Transgressions and the Problem with Making Fire
The Principle that Yom Tov Does Not Distinguish Categories of Melachot
Why Making Fire Cannot Be a Separate Lashing Category on Yom Tov
The Comparison to Other Cases Where Multiple Acts Require Separate Warnings

macro_segments.title: Solutions to Replace the Fire Category: Muktza, Avoda Zara Wood, and Hekdesh Wood

micro_segments.title: The Failed Attempt to Replace Fire with Muktza Wood
The Proposal of Using Avoda Zara Wood and Its Dual Prohibitions
The Final Solution: Using Hekdesh Wood as the Fifth Lashing Category

macro_segments.title: Connection Between Rava and Rav Chisda's Debate and Rabbi Eliezer vs Rabbi Yehoshua on Baking Tamei Challah

micro_segments.title: Rami bar Chama's Proposal: The Same Debate as Rabbi Eliezer and Rabbi Yehoshua
Rabbi Eliezer's Use of Ho'il: Each Loaf Theoretically Edible
Rabbi Yehoshua's Rejection of Ho'il: One Loaf Definitely Cannot Be Eaten
Rav Pappa's Qualification: The Scenarios Differ Based on Intent and Feasibility
Rav Sheshet's Counter-Challenge: The Debates May Not Be Identical

macro_segments.title: The Dispute Between Rabbi Yirmiyah and Rabbi Zeira on Whether the Debates Are the Same

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yirmiyah Accepts Rami bar Chama's Equation of the Debates
Rabbi Zeira Rejects the Equation Based on a Baraita Where Rabbi Eliezer Remains Silent
Rabbi Yirmiyah's Counter: Silence in the Baraita Does Not Prove Absence of the Defense

macro_segments.title: The Practical Halacha for Baking Tamei Challah and the Minimum Amount of Dough for Challah Separation

micro_segments.title: The Ruling that We Follow Rabbi Eliezer: Bake and Then Separate Challah
The Question of Minimum Dough Size: How Much Dough Before Challah Becomes Obligatory
The Measurements from Rabbi Yishmael: Two Kab of Wheat, Three Kab of Barley
The Disagreement with Rabbi Natan: Reversed Measurements Due to Fermentation Rates
Rav's Statement: One Kab as the Standard for Pesach and Challah Minimum
The Mishna's Requirement: Five Revaim of Flour Obligates Challah Separation
Rav Yosef's Solution: Women Bake Small Amounts but Combine in the Oven and Basket

macro_segments.title: The Role of the Basket and Oven in Combining Separate Loaves for Challah Purposes

micro_segments.title: Rabbi Yirmiyah's Question: Does the Basket Combine Loaves if It Has No Rim?
The Tannaim Dispute: Rabbi Eliezer Says Basket, Rabbi Yehoshua Says Oven
Rabbi Shimon ben Gamliel's Requirement: Loaves Must Touch Like Babylonian Breads

macro_segments.title: Practical Arrangements for Multiple Women Baking: The Assembly Line Model

micro_segments.title: Rabban Gamliel's Lenient View: Multiple Women Can Share One Oven Sequentially
The Rabbis' Requirement: Assembly Line with Three Stations to Prevent Chametz
The Three-Station Rotation: Kneading, Shaping, and Baking in Sequence
Rabbi Akiva's Principle: No Rules Possible; Observation of Dough Condition Required
Rabban Gamliel's Concession: Only the Principle of Inspection for Fermentation Applies

macro_segments.title: The Stages of Chametz Formation: Expanding, Se'or, and Siduq

micro_segments.title: The Expanding Stage (Tapach): Can Be Arrested with Cold Water
The Se'or Stage: Blanching and Light Cracking; Rabbinic Prohibition Only
The Siduq Stage: Crisscrossing Cracks; Biblical Chametz and Karet
Dispute Between Rabbi Yehuda and Rabbi Meir on When Chametz Becomes Biblical
Rava's Explanation: Visible Cracks Always Have Deeper Internal Fermentation
